narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 48.7% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, family background, and enrollment plans, focusing on patterns seen in past students without considering gender or race. The three biggest factors shaping this outcome were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 decile ranking, in that order of influence. Most notably, your strong performance placing you in the 8th decile for Year 3 grades worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward expecting you to pass, much like how top performers in later years often succeed on the bar. On the other hand, your LSAT score of 32—below the average of 37.27 for students who passed—pushed the prediction toward failure risk, as lower scores have historically correlated with tougher bar outcomes. Your Year 1 decile of 4, lower than the 6.04 average for passers, added a smaller but still unfavorable nudge in the same direction, highlighting an early academic hurdle. While your upper-middle family income aligns well with many who pass (about 45% of them), and your full-time enrollment matches the norm for successful students (over 93%), these didn't outweigh the key concerns. Compared to typical admitted students, your later-year strength stands out positively, but your LSAT and early grades fell short of their stronger averages, tipping the balance. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar profiles through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, strengthening your early academic foundation if possible through additional coursework, and reapplying with an updated profile that showcases your proven Year 3 momentum. Schools value growth stories like yours, so highlighting that trajectory in a new application could make a real difference.
